yes, there exists a real number $ x $ greater than 1 that is not an integer and satisfies the inequality $ \{x\} + \{x^3\} + \{x^5\} + \cdots + \{x^{99}\} < \frac{1}{2^{99}} $. to understand this, we must first recall the definition of the fractional part of a number, denoted by $ \{x\} $, which is the difference between the number and its greatest integer less than or equal to it. in other words, $ \{x\} = x - \lfloor x \rfloor $, where $ \lfloor x \rfloor $ represents the floor function. the fractional part of any real number is always in the interval $ [0, 1) $, and it measures the non-integer portion of the number. for example, if $ x = 2.3 $, then $ \{x\} = 0.3 $. in this problem, we are looking for a real number $ x $ that is not an integer and satisfies the given inequality involving the sum of fractional parts of odd powers of $ x $.

the key to solving this problem lies in the behavior of the fractional parts of powers of $ x $. when $ x $ is close to an integer, the fractional parts of its powers tend to be small, especially if $ x $ is slightly greater than an integer. this is because raising a number close to an integer to a power results in a number that is also close to an integer, and the fractional part is therefore small. for example, if $ x = 1.1 $, then $ x^2 = 1.21 $, $ x^3 = 1.331 $, and so on, and the fractional parts of these values are all less than 1. as the power increases, the fractional part may oscillate, but if $ x $ is chosen carefully, the sum of these fractional parts can be made very small.

consider the case where $ x = 1 + \epsilon $, where $ \epsilon $ is a very small positive number. in this case, $ x $ is not an integer, and it is slightly greater than 1. when raised to odd powers, $ x^n $ will be slightly greater than 1, and its fractional part will be approximately $ \epsilon \times n $, assuming $ \epsilon $
